title: Request Headers
subtitle: Attach metadata to your request — including authentication

sections.content:
Every HTTP request can carry **headers** — key-value metadata that travels alongside your request body.

Headers tell the server things like:
- Who you are (`Authorization`)
- What format you're sending (`Content-Type`)
- What format you want back (`Accept`)
- Which app is calling (`User-Agent`)

In curl, you add headers with `-H "Key: Value"`.
sections.analogy: Headers are the envelope your request comes in. The body is the letter inside. Some servers read the envelope before they'll even open it — if the envelope doesn't say the right things, your letter goes straight to the trash.
keyPoints: Add headers with `-H "Key: Value"` — can use multiple `-H` flags
`Authorization: Bearer TOKEN` is the most common auth pattern
`Content-Type: application/json` tells the server your body is JSON
Most public APIs require an Authorization header for private data

sections.instruction: Here's what the most common headers look like in a curl command.

blocks.label: Multiple headers
blocks.code:
curl https://api.example.com/data \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer my-api-key-here" \
  -H "Accept: application/json" \
  -H "X-Request-ID: abc123"

sections.explanation: The `\` at the end of each line is a line continuation — it tells the shell the command isn't done yet. It's just for readability; you can put everything on one line.

sections.instruction: First, confirm that the `/private` endpoint blocks you without an authorization header.

onSuccess.message: 401 — as expected. The server sees your request but refuses it without an identity token.

sections.instruction: Now add the Authorization header. The demo key is `student-demo-key`.
sections.hint: Use -H to add a header: -H "Authorization: Bearer student-demo-key"

onSuccess.message:
You're in! The server verified your token and returned the protected data.

This is how virtually every real API works — you register, get an API key, and include it as `Authorization: Bearer YOUR_KEY` on every request. The server checks it before deciding what (if anything) to give you.

completionMessage: Headers are how you authenticate, negotiate formats, and pass metadata. The `-H` flag is one of the most-used curl options you'll ever type.
